summary: Scan of AccountSyncService_Insecure.cls surfaces five canonical violations: the class lacks a sharing keyword (defaults to without sharing in trigger context), a callout uses a hardcoded HTTPS endpoint instead of a Named Credential, an API token is hardcoded in source, an Account update fires without prior FLS/CRUD enforcement, and a SOQL query lacks WITH SECURITY_ENFORCED / WITH USER_MODE. Two findings are P0 (hardcoded secret, callout-without-named-credential) and three are P1. Secret value is redacted in this report. Recommendation: rewrite via SecurityUtils + a Named Credential before this class touches a production org.
confidence_rationale: Source was fully inlined and all five canonical signals are unambiguous in the static text; MEDIUM (not HIGH) because the harness has no live org probe to confirm whether the hardcoded endpoint is already aliased to a Named Credential domain.

dimensions_skipped.reason: Single-file scope; no package.xml supplied so the cross-class blast radius could not be estimated.

findings.title: Hardcoded API token in source
findings.detail: AccountSyncService_Insecure.cls hardcodes a bearer token used for the Risk Scoring API callout. Secrets in source land in version control, sandbox refreshes, and any debug log that prints the variable.
findings.recommendation: Move the credential to a Named Credential. Reference it via `callout:Risk_Scoring_API` and let the platform inject the Authorization header — the Apex never sees the token.

findings.title: Callout uses a hardcoded HTTPS endpoint
findings.detail: Apex constructs the endpoint inline rather than referencing `callout:<NamedCredential>`. This bypasses the Remote Site Settings/Named Credential governance surface and means the credential cannot be rotated without a code change.
findings.recommendation: Provision a Named Credential `Risk_Scoring_API` with the endpoint and OAuth/Basic auth, then change the request to `req.setEndpoint('callout:Risk_Scoring_API/score')`.

findings.title: Class is missing an explicit sharing keyword
findings.detail: When invoked from a trigger or anonymous context, the class will run without sharing — exposing records the caller could not see directly.
findings.recommendation: Declare `with sharing` (default) or `inherited sharing` if the class is intentionally invoked from both trusted and untrusted callers. Document the choice.

findings.title: Account update fires without FLS/CRUD enforcement
findings.detail: DML on Account proceeds without `Schema.sObjectType.Account.fields.<Field>.isUpdateable()` or `Security.stripInaccessible(...)`. A user without FLS on a touched field will silently succeed at writes that bypass their FLS surface.
findings.recommendation: Wrap the records in `SecurityUtils.stripInaccessibleForUpdate(Account.SObjectType, records)` (see template) before the `update` statement.

findings.title: SOQL lacks WITH SECURITY_ENFORCED / WITH USER_MODE
findings.detail: The Account query reads fields without enforcing the running user's FLS. Returned data may include fields the user cannot legally see.
findings.recommendation: Add `WITH USER_MODE` (preferred on API 60+) or `WITH SECURITY_ENFORCED` to the query. If the query intentionally needs to bypass FLS, use `WITH SYSTEM_MODE` and document why.

process_observations.observation: Two P0 issues land on the same code path (callout without Named Credential AND hardcoded secret). The class likely cannot be deployed to prod until both are remediated together — a partial fix that only adds the Named Credential while leaving the token in source still leaks the secret.
After remediation, regenerate the Apex test class so callout tests stub through MockHttpResponseGenerator and FLS tests run via TestUserFactory.standardUser().
process_observations.followup_reason: because the rewritten class needs new tests covering Named Credential mock, FLS-enforced SOQL, and the with-sharing posture.

citations.used_for: FLS/CRUD enforcement pattern, with-sharing decision rubric, and the WITH USER_MODE preference.
Confirming the callout:<NamedCredential> idiom and the rotation/governance benefits over inline endpoints.
Reference scaffold for the stripInaccessibleForUpdate helper called out in the no-flsd-on-dml remediation.

followups.because: because the remediated class needs a fresh test class with Named Credential mocking, FLS-enforced SOQL coverage, and a System.runAs path.
because two P0 findings land on the same callout code path and a holistic review should confirm no other security smells remain after the remediation.
